fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that database pagination optimizer thing we discussed last sprint. The basic idea is that when devs do a bounded query to fetch a page of records, instead of pulling all the heavy row data upfront, we do a lightweight pass first to grab just the IDs, then fetch the full rows for only those IDs. Should work similarly to how we handled the deferred loading pattern in the billing module a while back — check how that was structured if you need a reference.

The tool needs to plug into our existing Rails/ActiveRecord stack (we're using SQLite for the in-memory test layer). It should handle the common pagination scenarios: plain limit, limit+offset, and also play nicely with that page-number helper we use on the backend (the one that also fires a COUNT query). Relationship preloading should still work but shouldn't bleed into the ID-selection step — that was a big complaint from the frontend team.

One thing that keeps biting us: devs sometimes forget to set any kind of page boundary, and then the optimizer just tries to run on the whole table. We need a clean, user-friendly error for that case — nothing gross with stack traces leaking out.

Also needs a test harness that can run the JSON-based test cases from a configurable directory and write outputs to per-case files. Make sure the structure is clean and not a giant single file — we've been burned by that before.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the core behavior is still the same two-step DeferredPaginationOptimizer / fast_page flow we talked about in Feature 1. For a normal bounded query that only has a limit, we should see exactly 2 SQL statements total: first a SELECT that pulls only the primary key IDs with the LIMIT applied, then a SELECT of the full rows WHERE id IN (the collected IDs). No extra statements should show up around that.

On the preload question, if something like preload_organization is requested, that still needs to stay out of the ID-selection pass completely. The order there should be: first SELECT ids with LIMIT, then SELECT full rows WHERE id IN (...), then SELECT from the related table. So total SQL count is 3 in that case, and the first ID query needs to stay clean with no JOINs or includes.

Also, for the missing boundary case, if there is no limit and no offset in the page request, this needs to raise the domain-specific error and not some generic runtime blow-up. The output needs to be exactly error=missing_page_boundary\nrequired=limit_or_offset\n with no stack traces, no host-language exception class names, and no extra fields.

For parity checks, the optimized path needs to return records in the same order and with identical IDs and login values as the standard non-optimized bounded query for the same limit/offset/order combo. That’s what compare_standard is there to verify side-by-side. And in the page-number adapter mode, when we use page + items, we also need to preserve the pagination metadata. So for page=2 with items=1, the result should carry current_page=2, next_page=3, prev_page=1 along with the right record.

Last bit: in backend_adapter mode from Feature 6, the pagination helper does a COUNT(*) first, and then we still do the same two-step ID+record fetch after that. So the full optimized flow there should produce exactly 3 observable SQL statements total.

trigger_keywords: backend pagination COUNT query
does the total count query still fire
backend_adapter SQL sequence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In backend_adapter mode, 3 SQL statements must execute in this order: (1) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "users", (2) SELECT "users"."id" FROM "users" LIMIT ? OFFSET ?, (3) SELECT "users".* FROM "users" WHERE "users"."id" IN (...). The COUNT query must not be skipped or merged. page_items, current_page, next_page, records_count, records_ids, and records_logins must all appear in output.

trigger_keywords: test harness output location
where are test output files written
how does the test runner name output files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes each case's raw stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt. For example, the first case in feature1_deferred_join_sql.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases goes to rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_deferred_join_sql@000.txt. Different --cases-dir values produce separate namespaced subdirectories so they never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: what database is used for tests
what ORM or query layer is this built on
in-memory database setup for the test layer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test layer uses an in-memory SQLite database via ActiveRecord (Ruby). The users table is seeded with a fixed dataset including fields like id and login. An organizations table is also present to support relationship preload tests. The SQL output uses double-quoted identifiers in SQLite/ActiveRecord style (e.g., "users"."id").

trigger_keywords: file structure requirements
should this be a single file or multiple files
how should the codebase be organized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must NOT be a single god file. It requires a multi-file layout with clear separation: core domain logic (the optimizer engine), an execution/test adapter (CLI entry point that reads JSON from stdin and writes to stdout), and the test harness script. The adapter is the only layer that touches JSON parsing and stdout formatting.

trigger_keywords: what fixed user records are seeded
what data is in the test database
which user IDs and logins exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The seeded users dataset must support the expected outputs: for newest_first order with limit=5 offset=5, IDs 5,4,3,2,1 with logins phani,frances,nicknicknick,iheanyi,mikeissocool must be returned. For backend_adapter page=1 items=5, IDs 4,7,8,9,10 with logins frances,derek,dgraham,ayrton,dbussink. For page_number_adapter page=2 items=1, ID=9 login=ayrton. The seed order implies a default (non-newest_first) ordering produces IDs like 4,7,8,9,10 first.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: relationship preloading shouldn't bleed into the ID-selection step
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2: when preload_organization is set in the page_request, the first SQL statement must only SELECT "users"."id" with no JOINs or subqueries touching the organizations table. The organizations SELECT appears only as the third SQL statement after full records are loaded.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a clean, user-friendly error for that case — nothing gross with stack traces leaking out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4: the missing_boundary_error mode must output exactly error=missing_page_boundary\nrequired=limit_or_offset\n using a domain-specific exception type, with no Ruby exception class names, backtraces, or runtime messages exposed in stdout.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'd need to dig into the existing codebase structure or check with the engineering lead. Try grepping the repo for relevant patterns or look at how similar features were handled in previous iterations.

feature1_deferred_join_sql.json.description: A bounded record query is loaded through a two-step pagination strategy that first selects row identifiers and then loads the matching rows.

cases.expected_output:
sql_count=2
sql=SELECT "users"."id" FROM "users" LIMIT ?
sql=SELECT "users".* FROM "users" WHERE "users"."id" IN (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)

feature2_relationship_preload_sql.json.description: A bounded record query that preloads a related collection keeps relationship loading out of the identifier query and still loads the related records afterward.

cases.expected_output:
sql_count=3
sql=SELECT "users"."id" FROM "users" LIMIT ?
sql=SELECT "users".* FROM "users" WHERE "users"."id" IN (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
sql=SELECT "organizations".* FROM "organizations" WHERE "organizations"."id" = ?

feature3_result_equivalence.json.description: Optimized pagination returns the same ordered records as the ordinary bounded query for supported limit and offset combinations.

feature4_missing_boundary_error.json.description: An unbounded query cannot use optimized pagination and reports that a limit or offset boundary is required.

feature5_page_number_adapter.json.description: A page-number pagination relation keeps its selected records and navigation metadata after optimized loading.

cases.expected_output:
records_count=1
records_ids=9
records_logins=ayrton
current_page=2
next_page=3
prev_page=1

feature6_backend_pagination_adapter.json.description: A backend pagination helper can fetch its page records through optimized loading while preserving page metadata and count-query behavior.

cases.expected_output:
sql_count=3
sql=SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "users"
sql=SELECT "users"."id" FROM "users" LIMIT ? OFFSET ?
sql=SELECT "users".* FROM "users" WHERE "users"."id" IN (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
page_items=5
current_page=1
next_page=2
records_count=5
records_ids=4,7,8,9,10
records_logins=frances,derek,dgraham,ayrton,dbussink
